data.title: Compass: Hold Rated As I Am Still Very Worried About The Risks
data.description:
Investment action

I recommended a hold rating for Compass (NYSE:COMP) when I wrote about it last September, as I expected an uncertain period ahead for COMP because of the various business risks I have identified. Based on my current outlook and analysis, I recommend a hold rating. While I like the stronger-than-expected execution, I am still very worried about the poor housing demand situation in the US, plagued by stubbornly high mortgage rates, and also the potential regulatory risks tied to COMP’s private inventory model. Given these factors, I believe maintaining a neutral stance is the most prudent approach at this time.

Review

COMP reported 4Q24 revenue of ~$1.4 billion, reflecting 25.9% y/y growth. The strong top-line performance was driven by a 24.1% y/y increase in transactions, significantly outpacing the broader US market’s 6.8% y/y growth. Organic transaction growth came in at 15.5% y/y, indicating that growth was not entirely acquisition driven. COMP also continues to win market share, with its current share increased to 5.06%, up 65 basis points y/y and 26 basis points sequentially. In terms of profitability, COMP reported adj EBITDA of $17 million, which was a sharp improvement from the -$23.7 million seen in 4Q23. For 1Q25, management guided revenue to $1.35 billion to $1.475 billion, and adj EBITDA in the range from $11 million to $25 million.

It was very pleasing to hear that COMP continues to gain market share. As of 4Q24, COMP has posted four consecutive quarters of y/y market share expansion, and I think a key reason is because COMP has managed to continue signing new principal agents, which has increased to 17,752, up 21% y/y growth, with retention remaining strong at 96.9%. I see this as a virtuous cycle, wherein COMP gains more market share, giving it more financial capacity to reinvest in the business (in tech or even potentially better commission rates) which helps it to attract more agents, which leads to more capacity to capture market share. Management expects to continue growing its agent base by 600-700 net additions per quarter, and if they are able to achieve this, I believe COMP is well on track to reach 30% market share in its top 30 markets by 2026 (management’s goal as noted in the 3Q24 earnings call). Winning market share (and share of principal agents) also means that competitors competitive positions weaken, as this is a zero-sum game—agents that work under COMP would mean that all potential leads belong to COMP.

COMP's private inventory strategy—where agents list homes as Private Exclusives or Coming Soon before they appear on the MLS (multiple listing service)—is also becoming a key differentiator. Currently, 35% of all COMP listings are either Private Exclusives or Coming Soon, and 55% of all new February listings had started as private listings. The impact of this strategy is a lot better than I had imagined. According to management, early results suggest that homes marketed under this model are receiving accepted offers 20% faster on average and experiencing fewer price reductions. Internal data also indicates that homes pre-marketed as Private Exclusives closed at an average premium of 2.9% compared to similar properties that went directly to the MLS. What I can infer is that this model has managed to create a “sense of urgency” for buyers. From the buyer’s perspective, if they really like the home, they have to get it now; if not, there could be more competitive buyers when it's being shifted to MLS. I still think we need to see a couple more quarters of performance to determine if this strategy works as well as it seems now. But if COMP can continue scaling this strategy, it could help heavily strengthen agent loyalty (since they earn more) and drive higher revenue growth.

Housing Market Weakness and Affordability Challenges

Despite COMP's strong execution, I am still very wary about the US macroeconomic conditions. In particular, I think the weak housing market will weigh on COMP’s ability to grow. Mortgage rates continue to stay at a stubborn level of ~7%, and no matter how good COMP’s strategy may be, it does not improve housing affordability, and a weak housing market means fewer transaction volumes. The Fed's messaging has also become increasingly conservative relative to last year. Just yesterday, the Fed meeting minutes showed that the Fed is looking for more progress on inflation coming down before cutting rates. We know from the latest inflation data that CPI has actually gone up. It seems to me that rates are unlikely to see major cuts this year, and that is not great for the mortgage rates outlook. The January 2025 home sales data, per the National Association of Realtors, also suggest a y/y decline, suggesting that housing activity remains sluggish. If mortgage rates stay elevated longer than expected, I believe it will dampen COMP's ability to sustain its current growth trajectory.

Regulatory Uncertainty Around Private Inventory Model

Another concern I have is regarding the potential for increased regulatory scrutiny of COMP’s Private Exclusive and Coming Soon strategy. The National Association of Realtors’ Clear Cooperation Rule mandates that most listings must appear on the MLS within one business day of being publicly marketed. While COMP has argued that enforcement has been inconsistent, a crackdown could force COMP to shift its strategy. Currently, management believes the rule is no longer enforced in nearly half of its markets, but this does not mean that regulatory will not come back and enforce it. If they do, it could limit the potential upside from this private inventory strategy.

Valuation

The market is seemingly focused on the potential upside and giving credit to COMP’s execution abilities. COMP’s forward EBITDA multiple has shot to its one-year high (19x), with the share price touching ~$10, a level it has not seen since late 2021. I am still not fully convinced that COMP's growth trajectory is clear. I think investors should not simply shake off the risks that I have mentioned above, especially the one about mortgage rates pressuring housing demand. If you recall what happened in September24, the COMP multiple also went up to ~18x, but valuation fell all the way back to 11x over the next three months. If the housing market demand situation gets worse, my fear is that this will repeat again as market participants become conservative on their growth expectations.

Final thoughts

My recommendation remains a hold. COMP has demonstrated impressive execution, with continued market share expansion, strong agent retention, and early success with its private inventory strategy. However, I still think the poor housing demand environment will weigh on COMP’s performance. The high mortgage rates and less likelihood of rate cuts by the Fed are concerning. Additionally, regulatory uncertainty around COMP’s private listings model could disrupt one of COMP’s key differentiators.

data.title: SentinelOne: Why I Am Buying More Now
data.description:
SentinelOne, Inc. (NYSE:S) is one of the most unique companies in the AI software space. What makes SentinelOne special is that it may be one of the purest AI plays in the lucrative global cybersecurity market. Another factor that makes SentinelOne very attractive is that it is an "under-the-radar" stock right now, and it has a relatively cheap valuation.

Moreover, SentinelOne will report earnings soon, on March 12th, and there is a high probability that SentinelOne could report and guide better than expected, in my view. Another element to consider is that SentinelOne recently caught a downgrade from UBS, which has caused its stock to slide, creating a compelling buying opportunity around the crucial $23-22 support area.

Technically: A Very Bullish Set Up

SentinelOne's stock has been beaten down recently, and it shed about 15% in 3 days following a UBS downgrade. We also witnessed a bullish reversal, as the stock bounced off the crucial $22 support. We could see a follow-through of the bullish reversal, and SentinelOne may move above $25 resistance to $30, and potentially higher, to about $35 by year-end. This increase would equate to an upside of roughly 60%, making SentinelOne a highly compelling buying opportunity in my view.

The Timely UBS Downgrade

While I and others may be bullish on SentinelOne, some aren't, and this is what makes a market. Roger Boyd from UBS recently slapped a downgrade on SentinelOne, citing some slower than expected growth metrics, the company's decision to reinvest in its business, and other factors. He decreased the price target from $30 to $25 on SentinelOne's stock, which may be the primary reason we saw the slide from $25 to $22 in recent sessions.

SentinelOne's AI Advantage

However, despite this negative note, SentinelOne's average price target remains around $30, representing the potential for about a 30% increase, in a base case outcome. Moreover, SentinelOne remains the AI leader in the cybersecurity space. Singularity is a top-rated cybersecurity platform, that enables unfettered visibility, industry-leading detection, and autonomous response.

SentinelOne may have the top AI-powered cybersecurity platform, which may perform the best relative to AI and other threats. This dynamic makes SentinelOne a top pick in the cybersecurity space, and we could see better than anticipated AI-powered efficiency and profitability ahead.

SentinelOne May Be Undervalued

It's crucial to recognize that SentinelOne is a relatively new company, founded just 12 years ago, and it went public around the height of the bull market in 2021, right before the savage tech bear market arrived.

SentinelOne traded up to a high of about $80 after it IPOed. In November 2021, its market cap eclipsed $20 billion. Given that SentinelOne had about $200 million in sales in 2021, during its peak, it traded at about 100 sales.

However, like many tech stocks, SentinelOne went through a tumultuous bear market, with its share price finally bottoming around the $13-15 range, and the stock hasn't climbed much from this ultra-low range yet.

SentinelOne's Valuation Looks Much Different Now

First, I want to point out that SentinelOne is going through a critical transformation stage as it prepares to transition to a sustainable, profitable company. SentinelOne could go from almost no or very little in EPS to roughly $0.20 cents in fiscal 2026, and it could double the EPS to $0.40 or more in fiscal 2027.

However, SentinelOne is only transitioning to just become slightly profitable. Therefore, it's challenging to use its potential P/E ratio to derive a valuation. Yet, we can look at sales figures. SentinelOne should have about $1-1.1B in sales in fiscal 2026.

Surprising for its growth prospects, SentinelOne's market cap is only about $7.8 billion. This dynamic illustrates that SentinelOne trades at only around 7 times sales. Please consider that this valuation is for a high-quality, high growth, AI-leading cybersecurity company in the lucrative AI software space.

SentinelOne Has Substantial Growth Potential

Due to its emphasis on growth and considerable expansion potential, SentinelOne could continue posting 25-35% YoY sales growth for several years as we advance.

With this type of growth potential, it is atypical to see this high quality/high potential stock trading at only 7-8 times forward sales, especially in this market. This phenomenon suggests that the market may be missing the bigger image regarding S, and is unjustly suppressing its stock price here.

Here is how SentinelOne stacks up against other cybersecurity stocks:

  • CrowdStrike Holdings, Inc. (CRWD) trades at about 24 times forward sales estimates, making it roughly three times as expensive as SentinelOne here. It's also expected to post lower sales growth than SentinelOne.
  • Palo Alto Networks, Inc. (PANW) trades at about 14 times forward sales estimates, making it about two times as expensive as SentinelOne here. Moreover, Palo Alto will likely only deliver about 15% sales growth in future years.
  • Zscaler, Inc. (ZS) trades at about 11 times forward sales, making it about 50% more expensive than SentinelOne here, and it's also expected to post slower sales growth than SentinelOne.

The Takeaway

We see a divergence here, as the highest growth potential stock also trades at the cheapest multiple. Therefore, SentinelOne appears undervalued here. A more fairer P/S ratio that would be more in line with the industry standards here could push SentinelOne's P/S valuation to about 15, equating to a stock price of about $50 in approximately one year from now.

Where SentinelOne's stock could be in the future

Due to its robust sales growth and profitability potential, SentinelOne may be capable of sustaining a relatively high P/E multiple as it becomes increasingly profitable in future years. Therefore, we could see considerable EPS increases enabling SentinelOne's stock to move significantly higher as we advance. This dynamic makes SentinelOne my top pick in the lucrative cybersecurity space for 2025 and 2026, with a $35-55 price target for the next 12-24 month period.

Risks to SentinelOne

SentinelOne faces risks despite my bullish assessment. The most considerable risk for SentinelOne may be the intense competition from CrowdStrike Holdings, Inc. (CRWD) and other top cybersecurity companies in this lucrative arena. SentinelOne also faces efficiency and profitability issues and must control costs carefully to keep up with EPS forecasts and continue expanding operations at a healthy pace. Investors should consider these and other risks before investing in SentinelOne's stock.

data.title: Sturm, Ruger & Company Vs. Smith & Wesson: Which American Gun Manufacturer Wins In A Shootout?
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Sturm, Ruger and Company (NYSE:RGR) (known as Ruger) and Smith & Wesson (NASDAQ:SWBI) are two American gun manufacturers with long successful histories and enthusiastic customers. Both companies have faced significant share price pullbacks recently, which has prompted me to analyze each company in an effort to determine which would make a better investment. I've ultimately ended up favoring Ruger over Smith & Wesson, although neither company is priced attractively enough yet to compensate for low growth prospects going forward.

Industry Overview

For a country so obsessed with guns, I was a bit surprised to find that America has just two publicly traded gun makers – Smith & Wesson and Ruger. While these two are the only investment options for those looking to capitalize on America’s enthusiastic gunslingers, they’re far from the only option down at your local gun shop. See the latest industry hierarchy in the chart below.

Ruger takes the top spot in volume with Smith & Wesson coming in third. Many names on this list, including Glock and SIG Sauer, are popular amongst handgun buyers but remain privately owned. While this article is focused on the two public companies on the list above, keep in mind that the industry is far from a duopoly.

Smith & Wesson was founded in 1852 by Horace Smith and Daniel B. Wesson in Springfield, Massachusetts. The firm has produced guns for the military going all the way back to the civil war and created cultural icons such as the .44 Magnum made famous by Clint Eastwood’s “Dirty Harry” in 1971. Tomkins PLC purchased Smith & Wesson in 1987 and entered into a controversial agreement with the Clinton administration to install internal locks on new firearms. Sales fell as a result of these measures and Tomkins eventually sold Smith & Wesson to Saf-T-Hammer Corporation in 2001. The combined company was rebranded back under the Smith & Wesson name and worked to regain the trust of the gun-buying public. More recently, Smith & Wesson in 2020 spun off its outdoor accessories business (American Outdoor Brands) and moved corporate headquarters from gun-weary Massachusetts to a more supportive environment in Tennessee. Today the company leverages its long history and reputation for reliability to support its brand power.

Sturm, Ruger & Co. was launched in a small shop in Southport, Ct in 1949 by William Ruger and Alexander Sturm. The new company’s first product, a .22 LR semi-automatic pistol inspired by the German Luger P08 and Japanese Nambu Type A pistols, became immensely popular due to its affordability, reliability and ease of use. The company has since expanded into the market for rifles and AR style weapons, with a proven track record of producing popular guns for a broad range of users.

Both companies focus on pistols, revolvers and rifles and have stellar reputations. My research covering industry publications, YouTube reviews and Reddit forums reveals two well-loved brands. For example, there are countless forum discussions where a poster is seeking advice on whether to purchase gun A from Ruger versus gun B from Smith & Wesson. The general consensus in most cases is that you can’t go wrong with either. If I were trying to tease out some difference between these company’s products based on online debates, Ruger may have a slightly better reputation for reliability while Smith & Wesson is perceived as a more fashionable and customizable choice. But again, the differences are very subtle, and I wouldn’t say either brand is clearly favored in the gun-owning community.

Balance Sheet Analysis

The firearms market can fluctuate wildly from year to year based on a range of factors including political developments, economic conditions and product innovation.

For that reason, companies in this industry are wise to maintain a conservative capital structure.

As shown in the table above, Ruger carries no long-term debt. Smith & Wesson, on the other hand, carries $100 million on a 6.92% revolving credit line as of their most recent 10-Q. Though their current assets ($306 million) are greater than total liabilities ($218 million), much of this is tied up in a swelling inventories account. This, combined with more than $150 million in costs related to the corporate relocation to Tennessee has created the need for them to draw on their credit line to satisfy the cash requirements of their business.

Firearms sales exploded during the 2021-2023 COVID boom, pulling forward purchases in a range of industries including firearms. Manufacturers struggled with mangled supply chains and strong demand throughout this period, creating a supply/demand imbalance that paired increasing prices with depleted inventories. Just as manufacturers were getting caught back up, demand dropped as gun buyers had satiated their appetite for new firearms. This created another supply/demand imbalance (heavy on supply this time) which is what these companies are dealing with today.

Still, both companies have strong balance sheets which gives them the ability to repurchase shares at fairly attractive valuations today.

Returns on Capital

Low debt and strong cash flow are great traits in a business, but withholding leverage can depress returns, and it’s important that management reinvest excess cash wisely. Let’s look at these companies' recent track record on this front.

Recent profitability metrics are very similar for this pair of companies. Smith & Wesson capitalized well on COVID-era demand in 2020 and 2021, while Ruger appears to have done somewhat better in the leaner period that has followed (and continues today). Ruger appears to have a stronger record on return on capital employed (ROCE) over the past decade, but Smith & Wesson’s returns may have been impacted by American Outdoor Brands prior to its 2020 divestiture. Also complicating a direct comparison is Smith & Wesson’s expensive relocation over the past 2 years. If either company has a clear advantage, it appears to be Smith & Wesson in gross and operating margins, which compare favorably to Ruger over most of the recent past. But this advantage disappears beyond the very recent history displayed on the chart above and neither of these measures take interest expense into account, which favors the debt-free Ruger.

Though neither firm stands apart on profitability metrics and returns have been “lumpy” (as opposed to the smooth line-up and to the right that Wall Street prefers), returns on capital have on average been quite good for both companies over the past decade.

Valuations

To this point, I’ve established that both Ruger and Smith & Wesson have strong balance sheets and generate adequate returns on capital. In this section, I’ll attempt to establish a conservative estimate of earnings power which may be used as a basis for valuation. The chart below spells out my estimate of mid-cycle earnings power for each company and is largely based on historical observations.

Capital expenditures and DD&A are roughly aligned for both companies in most years, so simple earnings-per-share (rather than Buffett’s Owner Earnings) are a pretty good measure of the value being created for shareholders on an annual basis. With an estimate of EPS in-hand, I’ll need to put some thought into future growth rates to determine an earnings multiple that I’m comfortable paying.

Revenue growth has been fairly lackluster for both firms over the past decade. As mentioned above, sales in the firearms industry can be lumpy - characterized by surges brought on by societal worry swinging towards pullbacks as Americans decide they’re packing enough heat. Revenue for both firms shot up in the wake of Barack Obama’s election in 2008 as gun owners feared stricter gun control laws with a democratic administration. Sales dropped again when Trump was elected in 2016 before spiking in 2020 as COVID and societal unrest boosted demand.

Looking forward over the next few years, there isn’t any obvious catalyst (e.g. election) that’s likely to drive sales up again. That doesn’t mean there won't be (nobody saw COVID coming), but it’s wise to factor low near-term growth into any estimate of valuation. That also doesn’t mean these companies can’t drive sales growth through their own inventiveness. New products represented a substantial 27.6% of Smith & Wesson's net sales for the 12 months ended April 30, 2024 according to the company’s most recent 10-K. Ruger recently reported that 31.2% of firearms sales for the first 9 months of 2024 were from products introduced in the past two years. That shows that these companies do have levers to pull to generate demand, but it may come in the form of increased market share as opposed to higher overall industry sales.

Certainly, there will be unforeseen stimulants ahead for long-term investors over the upcoming decade. But this seems like the type of low-growth situation where shares must be acquired at a significant discount for investors looking to generate high returns on investment. I’m not banking on compound annual sales growth of more than 4% in the decade ahead, followed but 3% growth thereafter. Under that assumption, and with a goal of earning 15% on my capital, I’d be willing to pay a multiple between 8-9 on the earnings of either of these companies. That corresponds with a purchase price range of $25-$29/share for Ruger and $6.80-$7.65 for Smith & Wesson based on my estimates of earnings power above. Unfortunately, each company would have to decline by over 20% from today's already depressed levels to reach my target purchase price range. Before adding these companies to my watch list and waiting for them to reach my target purchase price, I’ll first evaluate their management and the risks involved.

Management

As I evaluate management for these two firms, I’ll be focusing on specific actions each company's leadership has taken that demonstrate sound judgment. I'll start with a tangible example of competent management from Ruger that inspires admiration.

In 2020, the Remington Outdoor Company filed for bankruptcy - a stark reminder of why Ruger and Smith & Wesson are wise to carry little to no debt. The company was broken up and sold for parts, with Ruger acquiring Marlin Firearms for $30 million. Marlin makes iconic lever-action rifles and has a history going back over 150 years. The purchase price was relatively modest for Ruger (payable with cash on hand), and they quickly started designing and planning production of a bevy of new rifles under the Marlin brand. In the 4 years since then, Ruger has released dozens of new models to critical acclaim, with long-time fans of the brand very happy with the products that Ruger is releasing. Marlin devotees have expressed pleasure with Ruger's efforts to keep the brand name alive and continue improvement of the product.

Ruger’s management did a great job recognizing the value of a brand tied up in its parent company’s bankruptcy and pouncing on the opportunity at an attractive valuation. Though Ruger doesn’t break out specific sales figures for Marlin, there are a number of enthusiastic articles online singing the company’s praises on the rebirth of a beloved brand. The Company’s CEO, Christopher Killoy, is a Westpoint graduate with more than 2 decades of experience as an executive in the firearms industry. That’s an impressive resume, and it’s backed up by demonstrated competence with Ruger’s debt-free balance sheet, solid returns on capital, and the tangible example of a successful investment in Marlin.

Turning to Smith & Wesson, although the company has been fairly active in the years since CEO Mark Smith took over (2020), it’s difficult to tease out any specific conclusions on management performance. Smith initiated the relocation to Tennessee, which has reduced the number of manufacturing and assembly facilities from four to three. But it’s too early to determine the financial impact of this decision as it has happened during a particularly volatile period. The relocation was expected to cost about $120 million but has ended up costing closer to $160 million, a possible sign of mismanagement. I’m more inclined to attribute this to the skyrocketing construction inflation that occurred soon after the initial announcement was made. One mark against Smith comes in the form of Smith & Wesson’s need to take on debt to manage rising inventories over the past 2 years. Again, the relocation hasn’t helped the company’s cash position during this period, but Smith lacks the clean sheet in this regard that Killoy possesses at Ruger.

Risks

Both Ruger and Smith & Wesson have fairly low financial risk as a result of low debt levels and strong cash generation. But they do face a certain amount of legal risk. Ruger and Smith & Wesson are currently defendants in a number of product liability lawsuits involving the criminal misuse of their product. Gunmakers are protected to some degree by the Protection of Lawful Commerce in Arms Act (PLCAA), which was signed into law in 2005 and shields firearm manufacturers, dealers and importers from civil lawsuits when their products are used to commit crimes. However, this legislation will be tested this year as the Supreme Court decides on Estados Unidos Mexicanos v. Smith & Wesson Brands, Inc., et al. This suit was filed by the Mexican government against a handful of American gunmakers (including Ruger and Smith & Wesson) and essentially alleges that these companies produce and market firearms in a way they know results in illegal trafficking of firearms into Mexico. Gun manufacturers actually petitioned the Supreme Court to take this case because any ruling that weakened the PLCAA could expose them to a significant amount of litigation. Given the protection offered by the PLCAA and the Supreme Court’s conservative tilt, it’s unlikely this case succeeds. However, political winds swing from right to left over time and may eventually expose manufacturers to significant liabilities under the wrong circumstances.

Summary

Both Ruger and Smith & Wesson have strong brands that are popular with gun owners across the country. Additionally, both companies have shown an ability to drive sales through the development of new products as evidenced by the percentage of sales made up by new products. However, if you held a gun to my head (ha) and made me pick one of these companies for the purpose of long-term investment, I’d have to lean towards Ruger. The differences are subtle, but I’ve come away with a bit more confidence in Ruger’s management compared with Smith & Wesson's. Specifically, I find their management of the current industry pullback without taking on any debt and the successful rejuvenation of a brand acquired cheaply out of bankruptcy (Marlin) to be very impressive. Unfortunately, I don’t think either of these firms are priced at a level that yields a good chance at fantastic future returns given the fact that both are likely to be low-growth companies going forward. However, I’ve put both on my personal watchlist and will be watching for insider purchases and a pullback in price going forward. If the price becomes compelling and insiders start making significant purchases, I'll be an enthusiastic buyer.

data.title: Cobalt Miners News For The Month Of February 2025
data.description:
Welcome to the February 2025 cobalt miners news.

The past month saw cobalt prices significantly lower, hitting a multi-decade low.

Cobalt Price News

As of February 19, the cobalt spot price was at US$9.77/lb, lower from US$11.02/lb last month. The LME cobalt price is US$20,905/tonne. LME Cobalt inventory is 126 tonnes, flat from 126 tonnes from last month. More details on cobalt pricing (in particular the more relevant cobalt sulphate), can be found here at Benchmark Mineral Intelligence or Fast Markets MB.

Cobalt spot prices long-term price chart - USD 9.77 (Source)

Cobalt Demand Vs. Supply Forecasts

S&P Global Market intelligence - Cobalt demand vs. supply forecast as of Oct. 2024 (Source)

The global cobalt market balance is forecast to shift from surplus to deficit in 2027 (as of June 2024) (Source)

Morgan Stanley forecasts cobalt surpluses to grow this decade (as of Nov. 2023) (Source)

Trend Investing vs. IEA 2021 demand forecast for EV metals

2021 IEA forecast growth in demand for selected minerals from clean energy technologies by scenario, 2040 relative to 2020 - Increases Of Lithium 13x to 42x, Graphite 8x to 25x, Cobalt 6x to 21x, Nickel 7x to 19x, Manganese 3x to 8x, Rare Earths 3x to 7x, And Copper 2x to 3x

Cobalt Market News

On January 30, Miningdotcom reported:

Cobalt price plunge and the EV market...In a recent note, BMO Capital Markets pointed out that in fact real cobalt prices are at the lowest in history based on annual average prices adjusted for inflation going back to the late 1920s using USGS data. Nearly all cobalt output is a byproduct of nickel and copper mining. Copper production in the Congo is rising fast leading to a near 40% jump in the country's cobalt output in 2024. The DRC already accounts for 80% of global cobalt supply, estimated to have exceeded 300,000 tonnes in 2024.

On February 6, Miningdotcom reported:

Column: Another cobalt bust but this time it's different...But while it was an artisanal mining surge that caused the bust of 2018-2019, this time around it's China's giant CMOC Group. The company more than doubled its output of cobalt last year, pumping nearly 60,000 tons of extra metal into a global market of just over 200,000 tons.

On February 14, Fastmarkets reported:

Cobalt market faces oversupply hurdles amid China's demand watch and price challenges...CMOC's guidance for 2025 set at 100-120kt of cobalt suggests we're in for another bearish year for the market. In the US, the potential tariffs on Canadian cobalt metal could see short-term tightening on certain Western brands, however, we're not holding out for a strong recovery in standard-grade cobalt metal pricing for the rest of the year.

Cobalt Company News

CMOC Group Limited [HKSE:3993] [SHE:603993] (OTCPK:CMCLF) (formerly China Molybdenum)

No significant news for the month.

Glencore [HK:805] [LSE:GLEN] (OTCPK:GLCNF)

On January 30, Glencore announced: "Full Year 2024 Production Report." 2024 own sourced cobalt production was 38.2kt down 8% YoY.

On February 19, Glencore announced:

2025 Share buy-back programme. data.title: The State Of REITs: February 2025 Edition
data.description:
REIT Performance

REITs kicked off 2025 with a modestly negative January average total return of -1.29%. REITs fell short of the broader market as the Dow Jones Industrial Average (+4.8%), S&P 500 (+2.8%) and NASDAQ (+1.7%) all saw gains to start the year. The market cap weighted Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ) outpaced the average REIT in January (+1.65% vs. -1.29%). The spread between the 2025 FFO multiples of large cap REITs (17.3x) and small cap REITs (12.6x) widened in January, as multiples expanded 0.1 turns for large caps but contracted 0.9 turns for small caps. Investors currently need to pay an average of 37.3% more for each dollar of FFO from large cap REITs relative to small cap REITs. In this monthly publication, I will provide REIT data on numerous metrics to help readers identify which property types and individual securities currently offer the best opportunities to achieve their investment goals.

Micro cap REITs (-7.80%), which severely underperformed their larger peers in 2024, continued their losing streak in January. Large caps (+0.30%) and mid caps (+0.09%) eked out narrow gains, but small caps (-0.95%) averaged a modest negative return. After the first month of 2025, large cap REITs have outperformed small caps by 126 basis points.

7 out of 18 Property Types Yielded Positive Total Returns in January

38.9% of REIT property types averaged a positive total return in January. There was a 15.14% total return spread between the best and worst performing property types. Timber (+7.63%) and Industrial (+4.06%) had the strongest start to 2025. Single Family Housing (-7.51%) and Shopping Center REITs (-6.46%) started the year deep in the red.

The REIT sector as a whole saw the average P/FFO (2025Y) decrease 0.2 turns in January from 13.8x down to 13.6x. 50% of property types averaged multiple contraction, 44.4% averaged multiple expansion and 5.6% saw multiples hold steady in January. Land (32.1x), Data Centers (28.6x), Manufactured Housing (18.8x), Single Family Housing (18.3x) and Multifamily (17.1x) currently trade at the highest average multiples among REIT property types. Hotels (7.2x) and Office (9.1x) are the only property types that average single digit FFO multiples.

Performance of Individual Securities

Medical Properties Trust (MPW) (+18.73%) outperformed all other equity REITs in a very event-filled month for the health care REIT. MPW’s long struggling tenant Prospect Medical Holdings finally filed for bankruptcy on January 11th and on January 31st MPW refinanced large loans that would have matured in 2025 and 2026 with new notes maturing in 2032.

Creative Media & Community Trust (CMCT) (-66.22%) plummeted in January. CMCT announced a 1-for-10 reverse stock split on the first trading day of the year, but the share price continued to rapidly collapse down toward the pre-split price.

42.58% of REITs had a positive total return in January. REITs averaged a -1.29% total return in January 2025, which was actually a less painful start to the year than REITs had the prior January (-5.72%).

For the convenience of reading this table in a larger font, the table below is available as a PDF as well.

Dividend Yield

Dividend yield is an important component of a REIT's total return. The particularly high dividend yields of the REIT sector are, for many investors, the primary reason for investment in this sector. As many REITs are currently trading at share prices well below their NAV, yields are currently quite high for many REITs within the sector. Although a particularly high yield for a REIT may sometimes reflect a disproportionately high risk, there exist opportunities in some cases to capitalize on dividend yields that are sufficiently attractive to justify the underlying risks of the investment. I have included below a table ranking equity REITs from the highest dividend yield (as of 1/31/2025) to the lowest dividend yield.

For the convenience of reading this table in a larger font, the table below is available as a PDF as well.

Although a REIT’s decision regarding whether to pay a quarterly dividend or a monthly dividend does not reflect on the quality of the company’s fundamentals or operations, a monthly dividend allows for a smoother cash flow to the investor. Below is a list of equity REITs that pay monthly dividends ranked from the highest yield to the lowest yield.

Dividend News

3 REITs announced dividend hikes in January, 2 of which pay quarterly dividends and 1 of which pays monthly. Medalist Diversified REIT (MDRR) (+8.3%) announced the largest dividend increase followed by Postal Realty Trust (PSTL) (+1.0%) and STAG Industrial (STAG) (+0.7%).

A majority (53.9%) of REITs currently pay a higher dividend than they did pre-COVID (the end of 2019). 6.3% have the same dividend they did pre-COVID and 39.8% have a reduced or suspended dividend.

A number of REIT property types saw across the board dividend increases since the end of 2019. 100% of REITs have increased their dividend within the Single-tenant Triple Net, Casino, Land, Self-storage, Data Center, Manufactured Housing and Single Family Housing property types. However, none of the Mall or Outlet Center REITs have a higher dividend today than they did at the end of 2019.

Valuation

REIT Premium/Discount to NAV by Property Type

Below is a downloadable data table, which ranks REITs within each property type from the largest discount to the largest premium to NAV. The consensus NAV used for this table is the average of analyst NAV estimates for each REIT. Both the NAV and the share price will change over time, so I will continue to include this table in upcoming issues of The State of REITs with updated consensus NAV estimates for each REIT for which such an estimate is available.

For the convenience of reading this table in a larger font, the table below is available as a PDF as well.

Takeaway

The large cap REIT premium (relative to small cap REITs) widened in January and investors are now paying on average about 37% more for each dollar of 2025 FFO/share to buy large cap REITs than small cap REITs (17.3x/12.6x - 1 = 37.3%). As can be seen in the table below, there is presently a strong positive correlation between market cap and FFO multiple.

The table below shows the average NAV premium/discount of REITs of each market cap bucket. This data, much like the data for price/FFO, shows a strong, positive correlation between market cap and Price/NAV. The average large cap REIT (-4.80%) and mid cap REIT (-5.92%) trade at mid single-digit discounts to NAV. Small cap REITs (-23.27%) trade at a little over 3/4 of NAV and micro caps (-37.81%) trade at less than 2/3 of their respective NAVs.

Economic Health

2024 had the highest number of corporate bankruptcies in 14 years and January 2025 continued that painful upward trend. January 2025 had twice the number of bankruptcies as January 2024 (70 vs. 35) as high interest rates continue to take a toll on heavily indebted companies.

REITs Overall Saw a Slight Decrease in Short Interest in January, But Short Interest Remains Very High for Some REITs

Average REIT short interest remained largely flat in January as it declined by just 1 basis point to 3.8% of shares outstanding. Advertising REITs continue to average the highest short interest (5.9%) followed by Hotels (5.4%). Timber (1.8%) and Data Centers (1.9%) had the lowest average short interest at the end of January. Shopping Center REITs saw the highest increase in average short interest (+96 bps) in January, whereas Office REITs averaged the largest decrease (-65 bps).

Wheeler REIT (WHLR) saw the biggest increase (+11.2%) in short interest in January as the share price continues a multi-year freefall. Agree Realty (ADC) (+1.7%) and Creative Media & Community Trust (CMCT) (+1.6%) rounded out the top 3 short interest increases. The 10 REITs that saw the biggest declines in short interest were concentrated in 4 property types: Office, Healthcare, Industrial and Diversified. Net Lease Office Properties (NLOP) saw the biggest decline in short interest (-9.7%) followed by Medical Properties Trust (MPW) (-2.4%) and American Healthcare REIT (AHR) (-1.9%).

Closing a short position requires buying back shares in the position that was shorted. This means that companies with very high short interest can potentially experience strong upward pressure on share price when the short thesis materially weakens. A 2.4% reduction in the short interest of MPW helped propel the healthcare REIT to the highest total return in the REIT sector in January. With 35.9% of shares of MPW still held short and a whopping 16.6 days to cover, this heavy short interest could prove to be a strong tailwind if the short thesis fails to play out. Other REITs with high days to cover are Agree Realty Trust (ADC) (15.8 days), Getty Realty (GTY) (15.0 days) and Independence Realty Trust (IRT) (13.4 days). Heavy short interest can often be warranted by material risks and/or declining fundamentals, so it is important to fully understand these issues prior to initiating either a long or short position in any REIT. However, there is a potential for outsized gains when an investor correctly identifies which highly shorted REITs will not see the short thesis come to fruition.
